author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

ccel.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

title: Homily XLVIII on Acts xxii. 17-20.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, that, when I was come again to Jerusalem, even while I prayed in the temple, I was in a trance; and saw him saying unto me, Make haste, and get thee quickly out of Jerusalem: for they will not receive thy testimony concerning me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I said, Lord, they know that I imprisoned and beat in every synagogue them that believed on thee: and when the blood of thy martyr Stephen was shed, I also was standing by, and consenting unto his death, and kept the raiment of them that slew him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he thrusts himself (into danger), I came, he says, after that vision, “to Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I was in a trance,” etc. Again, this is without witness: but observe, the witness follows from the result.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said, “They will not receive thy testimony:” they did not receive it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet from calculations of reason the surmise should have been this, that they would assuredly receive him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For I was the man that made war upon the Christians: so that they ought to have received him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he establishes two things: both that they are without excuse, since they persecuted him contrary to all likelihood or calculation of reason; and, that Christ was God, as prophesying things contrary to expectation, and as not looking to past things, but fore-knowing the things to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then does He say, “He shall bear My name before the Gentiles and kings and children of Israel?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not, certainly persuade.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides which, on other occasions we find the Jews were persuaded, but here they were not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where most of all they ought to have been persuaded, as knowing his former zeal (in their cause), here they were not persuaded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when the blood of Thy martyr Stephen,” etc. See where again his discourse terminates, namely, in the forcible main point (εἰς τὸ ἱσχυρὸν κεφάλαιον): that it was he that persecuted, and not only persecuted but killed, nay, had he ten thousand hands (μυρίαις χερσὶν ἀναιρὥν) would have used them all to kill Stephen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He reminded them of the murderous spirit heinously indulged (by him and them).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then of course above all they would not endure him, since this convicted them; and truly the prophecy was having its fulfilment: great the zeal, vehement the accusation, and the Jews themselves witnesses of the truth of Christ!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he said unto me, Depart: for I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they gave him audience unto this word, and then lifted up their voices, and said, Away with such a fellow from the earth: for it is not fit that he should live.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews would not endure to hear out all his harangue, but excessively fired by their wrath, they shouted, it says, “Away with him; for it is not fit that he should live.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as they cried out, and cast off their clothes, and threw dust into the air, the tribune commanded him to be brought into the castle, and bade that he should be examined by scourging; that he might know wherefore they cried so against him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereas both the tribune ought to have examined whether these things were so—yes, and the Jews themselves too—or, if they were not so, to have ordered him to be scourged, he “bade examine him by scourging, that he might know for what cause they so clamored against him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he ought to have learnt from those clamorers, and to have asked whether they laid hold upon aught of the things spoken: instead of that, without more ado he indulges his arbitrary will and pleasure, and acts with a view to gratify them: for he did not look to this, how he should do a righteous thing, but only how he might stop their rage unrighteous as it was.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as they bound him with thongs, Paul said unto the centurion that stood by, Is it lawful for you to scourge a man that is a Roman and uncondemned?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul lied not, God forbid: for he was a Roman: if there was nothing else, he would have been afraid (to pretend this), lest he should be found out, and suffer a worse punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (See Sueton.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Claud. §25.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe he does not say it peremptorily (ἁπλὥς), but, “Is it lawful for you?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The charges brought are two, both its being without examination, and his being a Roman.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They held this as a great privilege, at that time: for they say that (it was only) from the time of Hadrian that all were named Romans, but of old it was not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He would have been contemptible had he been scourged: but as it is, he puts them into greater fear (than they him).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had they scourged him, they would also have dismissed the whole matter, or even have killed him; but as it is, the result is not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how God permits many (good results) to be brought about quite in a human way, both in the case of the Apostles and of the rest (of mankind).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how they suspected the thing to be a pretext, and that in calling himself a Roman, Paul lied: perhaps surmising this from his poverty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When the centurion heard that, he went and told the tribune, saying, Take heed what thou doest: for this man is a Roman.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the tribune came, and said unto him, Tell me, art thou a Roman?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said, Yea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the tribune answered, With a great sum obtained I this freedom.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul said, But I was free born.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then straightway they departed from him which should have examined him: and the tribune also was afraid, after he knew that he was a Roman, and because he had bound him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26–29.)—“But I,” he says, “was free born.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then his father also was a Roman.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then comes of this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He bound him, and brought him down to the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “On the morrow, because he would have known the certainty whereof he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him from his bands, and commanded the chief priests and all their council to appear, and brought Paul down, and set him before them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He discourses not now to the multitude, nor to the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Paul, earnestly beholding the council, said, Men and brethren, I have lived in all good conscience before God until this day.” (ch. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What he means is this: I am not conscious to myself of having wronged you at all, or of having done anything worthy of these bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then said the high priest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Right justly, and ruler-like, and mildly: “And the high priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him to smite him on the mouth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then said Paul unto him, God shall smite thee, thou whited wall: for sittest thou to judge me after the law, and commandest me to be smitten contrary to the law?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they that stood by said, Revilest thou God’s high priest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then said Paul, I wist not, brethren, that he was the high priest: for it is written, Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because “I knew not that he was high priest.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some say, Why then does he defend himself as if it was matter of accusation, and adds, “Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if he were not the ruler, was it right for no better reason than that to abuse (him or any) other?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says himself, “Being reviled, we bless; being persecuted, we suffer it” (1 Cor. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12); but here he does the contrary, and not only reviles, but curses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They are the words of boldness, rather than of anger; he did not choose to appear in a contemptible light to the tribune.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For suppose the tribune himself had spared to scourge him, only as he was about to be delivered up to the Jews, his being beaten by their servants would have more emboldened him: this is why Paul does not attack the servant, but the person who gave the order.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that saying, “Thou whited wall, and dost thou sit to judge me after the law?” (is) instead of, Being (thyself) a culprit: as if he had said, And (thyself) worthy of stripes without number.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See accordingly how greatly they were struck with his boldness; for whereas the point was to have overthrown the whole matter, they rather commend him. (infra, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For it is written,” etc. He wishes to show that he thus speaks, not from fear, nor because (Ananias) did not deserve to be called this, but from obedience to the law in this point also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And indeed I am fully persuaded that he did not know that it was the high priest, since he had returned now after a long interval, and was not in the habit of constant intercourse with the Jews; seeing him too in the midst among many others: for the high priest was no longer easy to be seen at a glance, there being many of them and diverse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So, it seems to me, in this also he spoke with a view to his plea against them: by way of showing that he does obey the law; therefore he (thus) exculpates himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.) (b) But let us review what has been said. (a) “And when I was came again to Jerusalem,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How was it, that being a Jew, and there brought up and taught, he did not stay there?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nor did he abide there, unless he had a mind to furnish numberless occasions against him: everywhere just like an exile, fleeing about from place to place. (c) “While I prayed in the temple,” he says, “it came to pass that I was in a trance.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (To show) that it was not simply a phantom of the imagination, therefore “while he prayed” (the Lord) stood by him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he shows that it was not from fear of their dangers that he fled, but because they would “not receive” his “testimony.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why said he “They know I imprisoned?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not to gainsay Christ, but because he wished to learn this which was so contrary to all reasonable expectation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Christ, however, did not teach him (this), but only bade him depart, and he obeys: so obedient is he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they lifted up their voices,” it says, “and said, Away with him: it is not fit that this fellow should live.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, ye are the persons not fit to live; not he, who in everything obeys God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O villains and murderers!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And shaking out their clothes,” it says, “they threw dust into the air” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23), to make insurrection more fierce, because they wished to frighten the governor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe; they do not say what the charge was, as in fact they had nothing to allege, but only think to strike terror by their shouting.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The tribune commanded,” etc. and yet he ought to have learnt from the accusers, “wherefore they cried so against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as they bound him, etc. And the chief captain was afraid, after he learnt that he was a Roman.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then it was no falsehood.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “On the morrow, because he would know the certainty wherefore he was accused of the Jews, etc., he brought him down before the council.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This he should have done at the outset.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He brought him in, loosed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This above all the Jews would not know what to make of.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Paul,” it says, “earnestly beholding them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows his boldness, and how it awed them (τὸ ἐντρεπτικόν).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then the high priest Ananias.” etc. (ch. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, what has he said that was affronting?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is he beaten for?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why what hardihood, what shamelessness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore (Paul) set him down (with a rebuke): “God shall smite thee thou whited wall.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly (Ananias) himself is put to a stand, and dares not say a word: only those about him could not bear Paul’s boldness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They saw a man ready to die * * * for if this was the case, (Paul) had but to hold his peace, and the tribune would have taken him, and gone his way; he would have sacrificed him to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He both shows that he suffers willingly what he suffers, and thus excuses himself before them, not that he wished to excuse himself to them—since as for those, he even strongly condemns them—but for the sake of the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Violating the law, commandest thou me to be beaten?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well may he say so: for to kill a man who had done (them) no injury, and that an innocent person, was a violating of the law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For neither was it abuse that was spoken by him, unless one would call Christ’s words abusive, when He says, “Woe unto you, Scribes and Pharisees, for ye are like unto whited walls.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, you will say: but if he had said it before he had been beaten, it would have betokened not anger, but boldness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But I have mentioned the reason of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And (at this rate) we often find Christ Himself “speaking abusively” to the Jews when abused by them; as when He says, “Do not think that I will accuse you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this is not abuse, God forbid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, with what gentleness he addresses these men: “I wist not,” he says, “that he was God’s high priest” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4, 5): and, (to show) that he was not dissembling (εἰρωνεύεται) he adds, “Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He even confesses him to be still ruler.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us also learn the gentleness also, that in both the one and the other we may be perfect.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For one must look narrowly into them, to learn what the one is and what the other: narrowly, because these virtues have their corresponding vices hard by them: mere forwardness passing itself off for boldness, mere cowardice for gentleness: and need being to scan them, lest any person possessing the vice should seem to have the virtue: which would be just as if a person should fancy that he was cohabiting with the mistress, and not know that it was the servant-maid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then is gentleness, and what mere cowardice?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When others are wronged, and we do not take their part, but hold our peace, this is cowardice: when we are the persons ill-treated, and we bear it, this is gentleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is boldness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again the same, when others are the persons for whom we contend.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What forwardness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When it is in our own cause that we are willing to fight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that magnanimity and boldness go together, as also (mere) forwardness and (mere) cowardice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that (does not) resent on his own behalf, will hardly but resent on behalf of others: and he that does not stand up for his own cause, will hardly fail to stand up for others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when our habitual disposition is pure from passion, it admits virtue also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as a body when free from fever admits strength, so the soul, unless it be corrupted by the passions, admits strength.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It betokens great strength, this gentleness; it needs a generous and a gallant soul, and one of exceeding loftiness, this gentleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or, think you, is it a small thing to suffer ill, and not be exasperated?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Indeed one would not err if in speaking of the disposition to stand up for our neighbors, one should call it the spirit of manly courage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that has had the strength to be able to overcome so strong a passion (as this of selfishness), will have the strength to dare the attack on another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, these are two passions, cowardice and anger: if thou have overcome anger, it is very plain that thou overcomest cowardice also: but thou gettest the mastery over anger, by being gentle: therefore (do so) with cowardice also, and thou wilt be manly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, if thou hast not got the better of anger, thou art become forward (and pugnacious); but not having got the better of this, neither canst thou get the better of fear; consequently, thou wilt be a coward too: and the case is the same as with the body; if it be weak, it is quickly overcome both by cold and heat: for such is the ill temperament, but the good temperament is able to stand all (changes).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, greatness of soul is a virtue, and hard by it stands prodigality: economy is a virtue, the being a good manager; hard by it stands parsimony and meanness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Come, let us again collate and compare the virtues (with their vices).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, then, the prodigal person is not to be called great-minded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How should he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man who is overcome by numberless passions, how should he be great of soul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is not despising money; it is only the being ordered about by other passions: for just as a man, if he were at the beck and bidding of robbers to obey their orders, could not be free (so it is here).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His large spending does not come of his contempt of money, but simply from his not knowing how to dispose of it properly: else, were it possible both to keep it and to lay it out on his pleasure, this is what he would like.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he that spends his money on fit objects, this is the man of high soul: for it is truly a high soul, that which is not in slavery to passion, which accounts money to be nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, economy is a good thing: for thus that will be the best manager, who spends in a proper manner, and not at random without management.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But parsimony is not the same thing with this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the former indeed, not even when an urgent necessity demands, touches the principal of his money: but the latter will be brother to the former.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, then, we will put together the man of great soul, and the prudent economist, as also the prodigal and the mean man: for both of these are thus affected from littleness of soul, as those others are (from the opposite).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not then call him high-souled, who simply spends, but him who spends aright: nor let us call the economical manager mean and parsimonious, but him who is unseasonably sparing of his money.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What a quantity of wealth that rich man spent, “who was clothed in purple and fine linen?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he was not high-souled: for his soul was possessed by an unmerciful disposition and by numberless lusts: how then should it be great?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Abraham had a great soul, spending as he did for the reception of his guests, killing the calf, and, where need was, not only not sparing his property, but not even his life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then we see a person having his sumptuous table, having his harlots and his parasites, let us not call him a man of a great mind, but a man of an exceedingly little mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For see how many passions he is enslaved and subject to—gluttony, inordinate pleasure, flattery: but him who is possessed by so many, and cannot even escape one of them, how can any one call magnanimous?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, then most of all let us call him little-minded, when he spends the most: for the more he spends, the more does he show the tyranny of those passions: for had they not excessively got the mastery over him, he would not have spent to excess.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, if we see a person, giving nothing to such people as these, but feeding the poor, and succoring those in need, himself keeping a mean table—him let us call an exceedingly high-souled man: for it is truly a mark of a great soul, to despise one’s own comfort, but to care for that of others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For tell me, if you should see a person despising all tyrants, and holding their commands of no account, but rescuing from their tyranny those who are oppressed and evil entreated; would you not think this a great man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So let us account of the man in this case also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The passions are the tyrant: if then we despise them, we shall be great: but if we rescue others also from them, we shall be far greater, as being sufficient not only for ourselves, but for others also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if any one, at a tyrant’s bidding, beat some other of his subjects, is this greatness of soul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, indeed: but the extreme of slavery, in proportion as he is great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now also there is set before us (πρόκειται) a soul that is a noble one and a free: but this the prodigal has ordered to be beaten by his passions: the man then that beats himself, shall we call high-souled?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then * *, but let us see what is greatness of soul, and what prodigality; what is economy, and what meanness; what is gentleness, and (what) dulness and cowardice; what boldness, and what forwardness: that having distinguished these things from each other, we may be enabled to pass (this life) well-pleasing to the Lord, and to attain unto the good things promised, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, to Whom be the glory for ever and ever.
